title: Welcome to GatewayOS2
author: Julian Lombardo
excerpt: Introducing GatewayOS2 — a bare-metal x86 operating system with a NeXTSTEP-inspired GUI, 50+ applications, and zero external dependencies.
content:
After months of development, GatewayOS2 is here.

This is a complete operating system built from scratch — every driver, every protocol, every pixel. No libc, no libstdc++, no frameworks. Just C++, C, and Assembly running directly on x86 hardware.

## What's Included

- **50+ applications** spanning productivity, games, security, and development
- **Full TCP/IP networking** with E1000 NIC driver, DHCP, and DNS
- **Java IDE** with recursive-descent interpreter
- **PE32 loader** for running Windows executables with 60+ Win32 API shims
- **Persistent storage** via ATA PIO driver
- **Compositing window manager** with draggable windows at 1024x768x32bpp

## The Philosophy

GatewayOS2 exists because the best way to understand a machine is to speak its language directly. No abstractions. No shortcuts.

The entire kernel compiles to roughly 100 kilobytes.

## What's Next

This is just the beginning. Future work includes expanded hardware support, AI integration, and continued exploration of what's possible when you build from nothing.

Check out the [source code on GitHub](https://github.com/julianLombardo/GatewayOS2) and try building it yourself.

title: Building a TCP/IP Stack from Scratch
author: Julian Lombardo
excerpt: How I implemented a complete networking stack — from Ethernet frames to TCP connections — inside a bare-metal OS with no libraries.
content:
One of the most challenging parts of GatewayOS2 was building the networking stack. Here's how it works, layer by layer.

## The Hardware: E1000 NIC

Everything starts with the Intel E1000 network interface controller. The driver initializes TX/RX descriptor ring buffers, configures the MAC address, and handles interrupts for incoming packets.

The E1000 is chosen because QEMU emulates it well and the programming interface is well-documented.

## Layer by Layer

**Ethernet** — Every packet on the network is wrapped in an Ethernet frame. The driver constructs frames with source/destination MAC addresses and an EtherType field that tells the next layer what protocol is inside.

**ARP** — Before you can send an IP packet, you need to know the MAC address of the destination. ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) broadcasts a "who has this IP?" query and caches the response.

**IPv4** — IP packets carry the actual data across the network. The implementation handles header construction, checksum calculation, and basic routing.

**UDP** — Simple, connectionless datagrams. Used for DNS queries and DHCP. No handshake, no acknowledgment — just fire and forget.

**TCP** — The complex one. A full state machine implementing the three-way handshake (SYN, SYN+ACK, ACK), data transfer with sequence numbers, and graceful connection teardown.

**DHCP** — On boot, the OS broadcasts a DHCP discover message to automatically configure its IP address, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS server.

**DNS** — Translates hostnames to IP addresses. Essential for the mail client to connect to Gmail's servers.

## The Result

GatewayOS2 can connect to the real internet, resolve domain names, and exchange data over TCP — all running on bare metal with no operating system underneath. Because GatewayOS2 *is* the operating system.

title: From TheGateWayOS to GatewayOS2
author: Julian Lombardo
excerpt: The evolution from a cyberpunk amber-themed OS to a NeXTSTEP-inspired system with 50+ apps — what changed and why.
content:
GatewayOS2 didn't appear out of thin air. It evolved from TheGateWayOS, my first bare-metal operating system.

## TheGateWayOS

The original was a cyberpunk-themed OS with an amber color scheme. It proved the concept — that one person could build a functioning operating system from scratch. But it was limited: basic graphics, minimal applications, no networking.

## The Rewrite

GatewayOS2 is a complete rewrite. Not a refactor, not an update — a new system built on the lessons learned from the first.

Key decisions in the rewrite:

1. **NeXTSTEP-inspired design** — Clean, professional aesthetics instead of the cyberpunk theme. The GUI system was rebuilt from the ground up with a proper compositing window manager.

2. **Networking from day one** — The E1000 driver and TCP/IP stack were core architecture decisions, not afterthoughts.

3. **Application ecosystem** — Instead of a few demo apps, GatewayOS2 ships with 50+ applications across every category.

4. **PE32 compatibility** — The ability to load Windows executables opened up possibilities no hobby OS typically has.

5. **Java IDE** — A recursive-descent interpreter running on bare metal. Because why not.

## Lessons Learned

- Start with the architecture, not the features
- Memory management decisions affect everything downstream
- A good window manager makes the whole system feel polished
- Networking is hard but transformative

The journey from TheGateWayOS to GatewayOS2 taught me more about computers than any textbook could. And it's only the beginning.
